Using:

```
$ python -m timeit -s 'import botocore.session; from botocore.handlers import validate_bucket_name; params={"Bucket": "my-bucket-name"}' 'validate_bucket_name(params)'
$ python -m timeit -s 'import botocore.session; from botocore.handlers import validate_bucket_name; params={"Bucket": "z9_.-" * 51}' 'validate_bucket_name(params)'
```

I get these results:

| Algo    | Bucket Name    | Time                |
|---------|----------------|---------------------|
| current | my-bucket-name | 1.79 usec per loop  |
| current | 'z9_.-' * 51   | 25.8 usec per loop  |
| set     | my-bucket-name | 1.67 usec per loop  |
| set     | 'z9_.-' * 51   | 16.3 usec per loop  |
| regex   | my-bucket-name | 0.971 usec per loop |
| regex   | 'z9_.-' * 51   | 2.67 usec per loop  |

So not much of a difference between set() and the current approach, but the regex version is faster for the worse case scenario.

I'll switch it over to a regex, and just include the regex in the error msg.

Not all newly introduced APIs have top level or second level navigation, such as Clojure and the Scala Infer API. I could remove Gluon, but frankly, I'd prefer to highlight Gluon here (and other places throughout the site) than have it as a separate top-level navigation item. That makes Gluon very much not equal, and IMO, it's confusing to new users.

Discoverability is important. Having more references and cross-links is good. People will be able to items listed in a different context and with a description. With just a navigation item I might never click on ONNX. But shown in the context of models, model zoo, or model server, I might. The same goes for the other topics like Gluon. The word "Gluon"  is used with the NLP and CV toolkits and as a Python API, and just by scanning the page, the user can see a better picture of what they can do with it.

BTW, I'm not saying no... just throwing out some counter arguments to consider.

```python

    b'\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x01\x00\n'
    b'dummytopic'
    b'\x00\x00\x00\x0c'
    b'testuserdata')
```

Is this example really doing what you think it's doing?

```python
>>> t = torch.Tensor([[0, 1, 1], [10, 0, 1], [10, 1, 0]]).view(1, 1, 3, 3)
>>> t
tensor([[[[  0.,   1.,   1.],
          [ 10.,   0.,   1.],
          [ 10.,   1.,   0.]]]])
>>> t[:, :, 0, :] = 0
>>> t
tensor([[[[  0.,   0.,   0.],
          [ 10.,   0.,   1.],
          [ 10.,   1.,   0.]]]])
```

This is zeroing out the top row, not the first column.  Is that what you expected?  And in general, I'd recommend using different numbers for every non-zero value, so there are no ties and it's more obvious what the MST should be.

Yeah, that would in principle work. Both `Ha.follow()` and `Ha.enforce_master_role()` call `Postgresql.is_leader()` and from what I can tell all relevant code paths end up there or `Postgresql.stop()` (via `demote()` or `restart()`). `is_leader()` should then catch connection errors and then check pg_isready that the server is indeed starting up, psycopg2 doesn't allow us to distinguish between server down and server starting up without parsing possibly localized error messages.

That said, I don't see a huge issue with checking for pg_isready on each iteration, but I do find loop-to-loop state propagation confusing and hard to understand as it is. Having something  called is_leader() change state to signal some other code about state change starts to get a bit too "spooky action from a distance" for my taste.

I'd actually like to refactor the code a bit to make state modification much more explicit and most of the code purely functional. The main idea is to have the main function take as input cluster state from DCS and current PostgreSQL state and have that output the desired action to take. But this idea is in pretty early phases at this point.

I see 2 approaches:

1. Update the time counter on every iteration of the loop in `forward_request`.
```
end = time()
time_left -= timedelta(seconds=end - start)
```
It is then fine to only check the nodes list once since it does not take a significant amount of time. If nothing is found the search will be simply repeated.

2. Pass the timeout (`utcnow() + time_left` in `forward_request`) to `pick` so that the while loop turns into `while current_time_ms <= connections[self.picked]['time'] and current_time_ms <= timeout`. Then you also need to alter `time_left` after the connection is found but before you make an HTTP request.

I think we need a better strategy than "raise ValueError" for cases where the back-end returns unexpected data.  The [Python library docs](https://docs.python.org/2/library/exceptions.html) describe ValueError as:

> Raised when a built-in operation or function receives an argument that has the right type but an inappropriate value, and the situation is not described by a more precise exception such as IndexError.

In these cases, there is no issue with values passed *into* a function or method: the issue is with data fetched from a back-end source.
